title: Using Cursor IDE Like a Pro: My Personal Guide to Building, Debugging, and Staying Sane
author: Vikas Ranjan
text:
Using Cursor IDE Like a Pro: My Personal Guide to Building, Debugging, and Staying Sane
“Cursor is like having a junior engineer at your side — the better you train it, the better it performs.”
Over the past few weeks, I’ve been diving deep into Cursor IDE, exploring how it can go from “just another code assistant” to a real productivity booster. I didn’t want to write another dry how-to blog. I wanted to document what worked for me — the tips, tricks, and habits I’ve developed while using Cursor in day-to-day engineering.
If you’re starting with Cursor or wondering how to really use it in production workflows, this one’s for you.
🧠 Think of Cursor Like a Junior Engineer
This simple mindset helped me the most.
Whenever I start a new task — say building an Order API — I break it down like I would for a new teammate:
- What’s the schema?
- What components are needed (controller, resolver, etc.)?
- What tests should we write?
- What design pattern fits?
Then I pass these in as small, focused prompts to Cursor. And it works beautifully. The more clarity and examples I provide, the better it performs. It’s not magic. It’s structured collaboration.
🛑 Save Yourself — Use Restore Checkpoints
Cursor has a feature called Restore Checkpoint, and honestly, it’s a lifesaver.
I often find myself exploring multiple paths — trying something, changing structure, updating logic. And sometimes things break. Instead of digging through undo history or checking git diff, I just restore a checkpoint from when things were stable.
It’s a simple feature, but once you start using it, you won’t stop.
💡 Don’t Overload Cursor’s Brain — Start Fresh
Cursor has a context window — and like all AI tools, once it fills up, responses get fuzzy.
When I feel Cursor’s losing focus, I just:
- Start a new chat
- Export anything critical from the previous thread
- Paste what’s needed to get back on track
I also add rules upfront like:
“Give short, direct answers. Skip filler.”
That helps reset the tone and clarity.
🧾 Use .cursorignore and cursorIndexing to Stay Focused
If you’re in a big monorepo, Cursor will try to read everything — even files that don’t matter.
I set up a .cursorignore file to filter out things like:
*.log
*.bak
config/*.env
node_modules/
If you want more control, you can use cursorIndexing to specify exactly what should be included. This keeps your AI focused and avoids leaking sensitive info.
⚙️ Set the Right Rules — Cursor Will Follow
Inside .cursor/rules, you can define how Cursor should behave.
Here are two rules I always include:
- Concise style:
- “Reply in a concise style. Avoid unnecessary repetition or filler.”
- No breaking changes:
- “Don’t introduce breaking changes unless explicitly requested. Ask when in doubt”
Treat these like a playbook. Cursor uses them to shape its output based on your dev culture and expectations.
🔌 MCP: Turning Cursor Into a Workflow Supertool
Now let’s talk about what really elevated my Cursor setup: MCP (Model Context Protocol).
This plugin system lets Cursor connect to tools like GitHub, Kubernetes, Figma, Jira, and even your database.
🔍 Real Use Cases:
- Queried a Postgres DB to debug permission issues using natural language
- Pulled Kubernetes logs to debug CI failures
- Reviewed and created GitHub PRs, directly from Cursor
- Used Figma Context MCP to convert design components to working React code
- Created diagrams and RFCs without leaving the IDE
It felt like having an AI-powered command center inside my editor.
🧪 Testing With Cursor — Frontend, Backend, All of It
Cursor fits perfectly with a test-first approach. I’ve used it to:
- Write unit tests and integration tests
- Generate Puppeteer scripts for frontend automation
- Add Selenium-based UI tests
- Trigger garden test suites
- Create backend mocks and test data flows
With the right prompt structure and a clear test goal, Cursor becomes your QA buddy too.
📋 Jira + GitHub Integration
With MCP, I added full Jira + GitHub workflows inside Cursor:
- Link commits to Jira tickets
- Create or update tasks directly
- Review PRs with context from issue threads
- Even automate test case tracking based on branches
This helped me stay in flow without bouncing across tabs.
🎯 Final Thoughts — It’s About How You Use It
Cursor won’t read your mind. But if you treat it like a capable but junior engineer, give it structure, rules, and guidance — it can do amazing things.
Use MCP to connect it with your real tools. Keep your prompts tight. Save your working states. Teach it with rules. And let it help you test, debug, document, and ship.
It’s been a game-changer for me. Hope it gives you a few superpowers too.
Please share your expeience with Cursor and let me know any new techniques.
About the Author
I’m a PhD research scholar and AI developer on a mission to transform innovative ideas into tangible solutions. You can find more of my work on GitHub and connect with Linkedin. Follow along for more AI magic!
